title: Book Review: A Blueprint for Worker Solidarity: Class Politics and Community in Wisconsin . By Naomi R Williams A Blueprint for Worker Solidarity: Class Politics and Community in Wisconsin. By WilliamsNaomi R. Urbana, IL: University of Illinois Press, 2025. 230 pp. ISBN 978025046247, $110 (hardcover); ISBN 9780252088322, $28 (paperback); ISBN 9780252047510, $19.95 (e-book).
abstract: Using Racine, a city in Wisconsin, as a case study, Naomi R Williams's A Blueprint for Worker Solidarity portrays the decline of the American Rust Belt as an aftermath of both an economic crisis and the undermining of working-class identity in postwar American society.Against this background, the author demonstrates the role of the workers' union as the engine of civic life, social solidarity, community solidarity, and watchdog for working-class economic rights.Over the decades, however, corporate restructuring, conservative politics, and deindustrialization gradually dismantled the benefits of unionism.Structured with an Introduction, six chapters, and a Conclusion, and based on archival records and extensive oral histories, Williams's book examines how women, Black workers, and civil rights allies adapted union strategies to assert their collective identity.In doing so, they made visible their shared power as workers while highlighting their distinct political, racial, and gender concerns.Concurrently, corporate restructuring, conservative politics, and deindustrialization compromised these workers' collaborative efforts.Moreover, the book demonstrates how resilience and fragility coexist by juxtaposing the strength of working-class traditions with their vulnerabilities to economic and political changes.In other words, the book highlights how the same local tradition of labor solidarity that allowed workers to organize and make gains also made them acutely vulnerable to economic and legislative attacks.This framing explicitly highlights the resilience and decline of working-class identity: resilience in the moments of successful collective action and solidarity, and decline when unions were weakened by strikes, corporate intransigence, and political restrictions such as the 1947 Taft-Hartley Act.Williams's work focuses not only on workers as workplace organizers but also on how they sustained their collective voices and power against external forces such as deindustrialization, the conservative backlash, and the structural constraints of post-New Deal legislation.This legislation, defined by the Taft-Hartley Act and the dominance of business conservatism, effectively depoliticized the labor movement, redirecting its militancy from broad social reform to securing only narrow contractual gains for workers.A Blueprint for Worker Solidarity achieves four objectives.First, it documents Racine's labor history and union activism.Williams depicts these happenings through the strikes of the early 1930s, during which workers lost their lives fighting for working-class citizenship rights.These sacrifices and collective historical memories strengthened the workers' commitment to labor rights and embodied a culture of militant resistance against entrenched corporate dominance in the postwar decades.Second, Williams examines the structural forces, such as plant closures and corporate restructuring, that eroded militant resistance.For example, William Jenkins and other activist workers shaped Racine's strong labor community by organizing unions and political actions.This legacy was severely tested by revolutionary events such as Local 180's clashes with J.I. Case's management (Case), which included a massive 440-day strike beginning in 1945.This landmark strike, which lasted more than a year due to the company's aggressive anti-union intransigence, reflected the intensity of the city's class struggles.The strike's immense material costs and union exhaustion ultimately constrained the union's ability and willingness to sustain similar aggressive, prolonged militancy in the postwar era.

title: Book Review: Feeding the Machine: The Hidden Human Labour Powering A.I . By James Muldoon, Mark Graham, and Callum Cant Feeding the Machine: The Hidden Human Labour Powering A.I. By MuldoonJamesGrahamMarkCantCallum. Canongate Books and Bloomsbury Publishing, 2024. 288 pp. ISBN 9781639734962, $29.99 (hardcover); ISBN 9781837261840, $14 (paperback).
abstract: Advances in artificial intelligence (AI) have sparked intense debates about automation and the future of work, yet often overlooked is the human labor quietly fueling these technologies.Feeding the Machine: The Hidden Human Labour Powering A.I. by James Muldoon, Mark Graham, and Callum Cant directly addresses this oversight.The authors deliver a timely exposé of the workers and workplaces behind AI's glossy façade.For scholars of labor and employment, the book's focus on how AI systems rely on global networks of precarious labor makes it a highly relevant contribution.It situates AI not as a purely technical marvel but as a phenomenon deeply embedded in patterns of labor exploitation, a perspective that enriches ongoing discussions in labor studies about the gig economy, platform work, and the political economy of technology.At its core, Feeding the Machine argues that AI is not a futuristic autonomous force but instead an "extraction machine" built on human toil.Drawing on more than 200 interviews conducted across more than a decade of research, the authors reveal a vast, intricate network of labor that underpins AI-a network that spans from low-wage data annotators to well-paid engineers, all operating within a global division of labor reminiscent of colonial-era exploitation.The book connects the hidden workforce of AI to long-standing histories of gendered, racialized, and geographic inequalities, asserting that today's AI boom echoes earlier industrial and colonial labor regimes.Rather than organizing the material by technology or industry, the authors structure the narrative around seven representative human figures in the AI production pipeline.These include the annotator, the engineer, the technician, the artist, the operator, the investor, and the organizer.Each figure is drawn from real-world cases and ethnographic interviews, providing a window into a different link in the AI labor chain.Through these portraits, the book's overarching narrative illustrates how disparate workers are interconnected in serving the AI industry's needs.For example, we meet a Ugandan data annotator performing monotonous data-labeling tasks for pennies.By contrast, an AI engineer in a gleaming tech hub enjoys comfortable pay and perks, yet her creative efforts to build new algorithms ultimately depend on the invisible labor feeding the data sets behind the scenes.The organizer figure, a Kenyan content moderator-turned-activist, exemplifies resistance: After enduring traumatic work filtering toxic online content, he helps spark the formation of Africa's first union for AI data workers.These and other accounts (including an Irish voice artist who discovers her voice was cloned by an algorithm without consent) vividly demonstrate the human faces of AI's supply chain.Crucially, the authors show that the actions of one set of actors can drastically affect others: A Silicon Valley investor's push for profit and speed trickles down to tighter deadlines for annotators, and an Amazon warehouse operator is managed by AIdriven systems designed by faraway engineers and sanctioned by corporate strategists.The central thesis is thus clear and powerful: AI is a socio-technical system sustained by exploited human labor around the globe.Feeding the Machine is a valuable and provocative study, but it is not without its shortcomings.A first critique is that several chapters devote excessive attention to technical and systemic descriptions at the expense of fully developing the workers' lived experiences.The authors' background explanations-for example, a detailed digression into Amazon's warehouse 1426904I LRXXX10.

title: Book Review: Focusing or Fragmenting Representation at Work? Specialist Trade Union Representation in the United Kingdom . By Andy Hodder and Miguel Martínez Lucio Focusing or Fragmenting Representation at Work? Specialist Trade Union Representation in the United Kingdom. Edited by HodderAndyLucioMiguel Martínez. Leeds, England, UK: Emerald Publishing, 2025. 146 pp. ISBN 9781801174831, $105 (hardcover); ISBN 9781801174824, $105 (e-book).
